title: The case for cities built around staircases
summary: Argue whether hilly cities should preserve their staircase-based street life or smooth themselves out with funiculars, lifts, and ramps.
topic: Whether vertical, staircase-rich cities should be defended against the pressure to flatten themselves
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether cities built on steep terrain — Tbilisi, Naples, Lisbon, Hong Kong's mid-levels — should preserve their characteristic staircase-based street life, or whether the case for accessibility (lifts, ramps, funiculars, escalators) should override that texture. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Anchor the argument in at least one named city, neighbourhood, or staircase. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What a staircase quietly produces in a city that an escalator can never replicate is…
Granted, defending stairs in the abstract is easy for someone who has never had to negotiate them with a pram, a shopping bag, or a…
Climbing the long stepped streets of the Sololaki district in Tbilisi on a summer evening, one notices that the social life of the neighbourhood does not happen on the…

địa hình của một thành phố

hintVocab.phrase: to design for the obvious user

cấu trúc tế vi của đô thị
có thể đi lại được bằng chân
cuộc gặp tình cờ

sampleText:
A city built on a hill does not have streets in the same sense that a flat city does. It has staircases that happen to function as streets. The long stepped passages of Tbilisi's Sololaki district, the stairways of Lisbon's Alfama, the narrow flights climbing out of Naples' Spanish Quarter — these are not detours between the proper roads, they are the roads. To understand a hill city, one has to understand the social life that happens on its stairs.

What a staircase quietly produces in a city that an escalator can never replicate is a particular tempo of public encounter. People climb at walking pace, stop on landings, lean against the railings, set down their shopping for a moment, exchange a sentence with whoever they meet coming down. Children play on the steps because they are the most usable flat surfaces in the neighbourhood. The stairs are not merely circulation; they are the neighbourhood's living room.

Granted, defending stairs in the abstract is easy for someone who has never had to negotiate them with a pram, a wheelchair, a heart condition, or three bags of groceries. The case for accessibility is not a romantic preference; it is a real claim made by real residents who are quietly excluded by the current arrangement. An elderly woman who has lived on the fifth flight of stairs for forty years should not be reduced to making one trip a week because nobody has built a lift she can use.

Even so, the choice between preserving the stairs and providing access is largely a false one. What thoughtful hill-city planning looks like, in practice, is something more layered: outdoor lifts and small funiculars added discreetly at key points, ramped alternatives where the gradient allows, lit handrails along the steeper flights, and resting benches built into the longer climbs. The staircase remains. The neighbourhood becomes navigable.

Admittedly, the temptation to flatten the topography entirely is strong, particularly when developers are involved. A new tower with its own private lifts can be sold to its residents as a complete neighbourhood; the stairways outside become someone else's problem. This is the wrong direction. It does not solve the access question for the people who already live on the hill; it simply replaces the hill with something else.

The case for the staircase city is not nostalgic. It is, in the end, a case for the particular kind of public life that a stepped street produces — the incidental encounter, the slow climb, the shared landing — and a refusal to surrender that life on the assumption that the only solution to a hill is to pretend it isn't there.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to name an irreplaceable social effect
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a staircase quietly produces in a city that an escalator can never replicate is a particular tempo of public encounter' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để đặt một hệ quả xã hội mà bình thường khó diễn đạt vào vị trí được nhấn mạnh. Mệnh đề quan hệ chèn vào 'that an escalator can never replicate' tạo ra sự đối chiếu sắc nét với một công nghệ thay thế.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a staircase quietly produces in a city that an escalator can never replicate is a particular tempo of public encounter' uses a what-cleft to place a hard-to-name social effect into the sentence's emphatic position. The embedded relative clause 'that an escalator can never replicate' sets up a sharp contrast with a replacement technology.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Semicolon-balanced contrast for redefinition
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The stairs are not merely circulation; they are the neighbourhood's living room' dùng dấu chấm phẩy để cân bằng hai mệnh đề đối lập chặt chẽ. Cấu trúc song song 'are not merely X; they are Y' tạo ra một sự tái định nghĩa mạnh mẽ, và phép ẩn dụ 'living room' nén toàn bộ luận điểm vào một danh từ duy nhất.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The stairs are not merely circulation; they are the neighbourhood's living room' uses a semicolon to balance two tightly contrasted clauses. The parallel 'are not merely X; they are Y' produces a forceful redefinition, and the metaphor 'living room' compresses the whole argument into a single noun.

sampleTranslationVi:
Một thành phố được xây trên đồi không có những con phố theo cùng nghĩa như một thành phố bằng phẳng. Nó có những cầu thang tình cờ hoạt động như những con phố. Những lối đi dài có bậc của khu Sololaki ở Tbilisi, những cầu thang của khu Alfama ở Lisbon, những đoạn dốc hẹp leo lên từ Khu Tây Ban Nha của Naples — những thứ này không phải là đường vòng giữa các con đường chính, chúng chính là những con đường. Để hiểu một thành phố đồi, người ta phải hiểu cuộc sống xã hội diễn ra trên những cầu thang của nó.

Điều mà một cầu thang lặng lẽ tạo ra trong một thành phố mà một thang cuốn không bao giờ có thể tái tạo lại là một nhịp độ đặc biệt của sự gặp gỡ công cộng. Người ta leo ở tốc độ đi bộ, dừng lại trên các chiếu nghỉ, dựa vào tay vịn, đặt túi mua sắm xuống một lúc, trao đổi một câu với bất kỳ ai họ gặp đang xuống. Trẻ em chơi trên các bậc thang vì chúng là những bề mặt phẳng có thể sử dụng nhất trong khu phố. Các bậc thang không chỉ đơn thuần là sự lưu thông; chúng là phòng khách của khu phố.

Thừa nhận rằng, bảo vệ cầu thang một cách trừu tượng thì dễ đối với người chưa bao giờ phải đối mặt với chúng cùng một chiếc xe đẩy, một xe lăn, một bệnh tim, hay ba túi tạp hóa. Lập luận về khả năng tiếp cận không phải là một sở thích lãng mạn; đó là một yêu cầu thực sự được đưa ra bởi những cư dân thực sự đang âm thầm bị loại trừ bởi sự sắp xếp hiện tại. Một người phụ nữ lớn tuổi đã sống ở tầng thứ năm của cầu thang trong bốn mươi năm không nên bị thu nhỏ thành chỉ có thể đi một chuyến mỗi tuần vì không ai xây thang máy mà bà có thể sử dụng.

Dẫu vậy, sự lựa chọn giữa việc bảo tồn cầu thang và việc cung cấp khả năng tiếp cận phần lớn là một lựa chọn sai. Quy hoạch thành phố đồi chu đáo trông như thế nào, trong thực tế, là điều gì đó nhiều lớp hơn: thang máy ngoài trời và các đường sắt leo dốc nhỏ được thêm vào một cách kín đáo tại các điểm chính, các phương án bằng đường dốc nơi độ dốc cho phép, tay vịn được chiếu sáng dọc theo các đoạn dốc hơn, và ghế nghỉ được xây vào các đoạn leo dài hơn. Cầu thang vẫn còn. Khu phố trở nên có thể đi lại được.

Thừa nhận rằng, sự cám dỗ san phẳng hoàn toàn địa hình là mạnh mẽ, đặc biệt khi các nhà phát triển tham gia. Một tòa tháp mới với thang máy riêng có thể được bán cho cư dân như một khu phố hoàn chỉnh; những cầu thang bên ngoài trở thành vấn đề của người khác. Đây là hướng đi sai. Nó không giải quyết câu hỏi về khả năng tiếp cận cho những người đã sống trên đồi; nó chỉ đơn giản là thay thế ngọn đồi bằng một thứ khác.

Lập luận về thành phố cầu thang không phải là hoài niệm. Cuối cùng, đó là một lập luận về loại sinh hoạt công cộng đặc biệt mà một con đường có bậc tạo ra — cuộc gặp tình cờ, sự leo chậm, sàn chung — và một sự từ chối giao nộp cuộc sống đó dựa trên giả định rằng giải pháp duy nhất cho một ngọn đồi là giả vờ nó không có ở đó.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to name what stairs uniquely produce?
options: A tempo of public encounter what a staircase produces never can an escalator replicate.
What a staircase quietly produces in a city that an escalator can never replicate is a particular tempo of public encounter.
An escalator never replicate can what a staircase quietly produces a tempo public encounter.
Replicate never can escalator what staircase produces particular tempo public encounter.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' places 'a particular tempo of public encounter' in the emphatic position; the embedded relative clause 'that an escalator can never replicate' sharpens the contrast.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the key noun phrase placed after 'is'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'topography of a city' mean?
options: The legal map of a city's administrative boundaries
The physical shape and surface features of a city's terrain — hills, valleys, slopes
The way a city's name appears on signs and printed maps
The arrangement of a city's main streets
mcQuestions.explanation: Topography is the physical surface of an area — its rises, falls, slopes — which shape how that area can be walked, built on, or traversed.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'topo' as relating to physical land surface, not human administration.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'granted' correctly as a concession?
options: Granted, defending stairs in the abstract is easy for someone who has never had to negotiate them with a pram.
Defending stairs granted easy in the abstract is for someone never had to with a pram negotiate them who.
In the abstract granted easy is defending stairs for someone with a pram never had to negotiate them who.
Negotiate them with a pram never granted had to who someone for is easy defending stairs in the abstract.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause cleanly opens a concession to the opposing argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full, grammatical sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a passive construction to foreground who is excluded?
options: Real residents who are quietly excluded by the current arrangement.
The current arrangement excludes real residents who quietly are.
Quietly the current arrangement excludes real residents who by.
By the current arrangement quietly excluded real residents who are.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'are quietly excluded by the current arrangement' is a passive clause that places 'residents' in subject position, foregrounding the people being excluded rather than the system doing the excluding.
mcQuestions.hint: Which sentence puts 'residents' (not 'arrangement') in subject position?

options: The extent to which a neighbourhood absorbs rainwater into its soil
The ease with which people can move through and across a neighbourhood on foot
A neighbourhood's openness to new immigrants
The acoustic quality of a neighbourhood's streets
mcQuestions.explanation: In urbanism, permeability describes how easily pedestrians can pass through a neighbourhood — through streets, lanes, stairways — rather than being blocked by walls, gated developments, or impassable terrain.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'permeable' as 'easy to pass through' applied to urban form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'they are the neighbourhood's living room'?
options: The stairs contain literal furniture from residents' living rooms.
The stairs serve as the neighbourhood's shared social space where daily life unfolds.
The stairs are where new residents move in when they arrive.
The stairs are the only public space heated in winter.
mcQuestions.explanation: The metaphor presents the staircase as functioning like a living room — a shared social space where neighbours pause, talk, watch children, and conduct daily life.
mcQuestions.hint: What kind of activity happens in a 'living room'? Apply that to a staircase.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to be navigable on foot' mean?
options: To be accessible only by hiring a foot guide
To be possible to traverse by walking, including for people with mobility limitations
To be approved by the foot health department
To require special footwear to enter
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Navigable on foot' means walkable — and in the essay's argument, walkable for people across a range of abilities, not just the unencumbered young.
mcQuestions.hint: What does it mean for a route to be 'navigable' — possible to travel through?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a participle clause to describe planning interventions?
options: Outdoor lifts and small funiculars added discreetly at key points.
Added discreetly outdoor lifts and small funiculars at key points.
Key points at outdoor lifts and small funiculars discreetly added.
Small funiculars outdoor lifts discreetly added at key points and.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'added discreetly at key points' is a reduced past-participle clause modifying 'outdoor lifts and small funiculars', describing how they have been installed.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a past-participle phrase ('added discreetly…') that follows the noun and describes a quality of installation.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example,
Even so, the choice between preserving the stairs and providing access is largely a false one.
On the contrary, the staircase is the only solution.
Furthermore, all stairs must be replaced.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the previous concession (the access problem is real) and pivots to the main argument that the choice is not binary.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reframes the question?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: All stairs should be preserved and access requests should be ignored.
All hill cities should be flattened to provide universal access.
Hill cities should layer accessibility infrastructure onto the existing stair network without erasing the staircase city.
Hill cities should be replaced by tower developments with private lifts.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues for a layered solution: outdoor lifts, funiculars, ramps where possible, lit rails, benches — all added discreetly so the staircase city remains.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the fourth paragraph for the essay's clearest statement of its preferred approach.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly stairs are way cooler than escalators and people who disagree just don't get it.
Hill cities are basically all about the stairs and we shouldn't mess with them.
The staircase, as an organising element of the hill city, supports forms of social interaction that mechanised vertical circulation cannot replicate.
Stairs are pretty important and we should keep them but also help old people, you know?
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('organising element', 'social interaction', 'mechanised vertical circulation') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal intensifiers.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'incidental encounter' mean in this essay?
options: A planned meeting between two neighbours
An unplanned, brief social meeting that occurs as a by-product of moving through public space
An accident on a staircase
A medical emergency that happens by chance
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Incidental encounters' are the brief, unplanned meetings — a nod, a sentence, a moment of acknowledgement — that happen because people share physical space, and which staircases produce more of than escalators do.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'incidental' mean — planned or accidental?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a defining relative clause?
options: An elderly woman who has lived on the fifth flight of stairs for forty years should not be reduced to making one trip a week.
Who has lived on the fifth flight for forty years an elderly woman should not be reduced to one trip a week making.
Should not be reduced to making one trip a week an elderly woman for forty years who has lived.
Forty years on the fifth flight an elderly woman who lived has reduced to one trip should not be making.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'who has lived on the fifth flight of stairs for forty years' is a defining relative clause specifying which elderly woman the essay means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'who', 'that', or 'which'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to surrender that life' mean in the closing paragraph?
options: To formally hand over one's home to a developer
To give up the particular kind of social existence the staircase city makes possible
To stop living in the city and move to a flatter area
To submit one's life story to a tourist publication
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Surrender' here is a moral verb: giving up something valuable rather than handing it over physically. What is being surrendered is the kind of public life the stair city produces.
mcQuestions.hint: What is being given up, and on what assumption?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best closes the essay by naming the false assumption it rejects?
options: In conclusion, stairs are important to many hill cities.
Therefore, all hill cities should immediately install funiculars.

The case for the staircase city is not nostalgic. It is, in the end, a case for the particular kind of public life that a stepped street produces — and a refusal to surrender that life on the assumption that the only solution to a hill is to pretend it isn't there.
To sum up, hill cities and flat cities are different from each other.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C explicitly names the wrong assumption ('the only solution to a hill is to pretend it isn't there') and rejects it — a strong rhetorical close.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends by naming a specific bad assumption rather than offering a vague summary?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say a new tower with private lifts does to the access question?
options: It solves it for everyone who lives on the hill.
It does not solve it for existing hill residents; it simply replaces the hill with something else.
It makes the hill more accessible to elderly residents in the surrounding streets.
It is the cheapest way to provide universal access.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay treats private-tower development as a non-solution: it provides access for its own buyers but does nothing for the people who already live on the slope.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph five, which discusses the tower solution.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a place in the city you live in or have visited that depends on stairs, slopes, or other physical irregularities. What would change about daily life there if a developer flattened or smoothed it tomorrow? Who would gain, and who would quietly lose?
